challenge_text: Start the broker. Subscribe two clients to a topic, leave a third on a different topic. Publish one message. Confirm exactly the two reach it and the third does not. SUCCESS: both same-topic subs receive intact; off-topic sub receives nothing; no error output.

friction_points.description: No subscriber-ready signal: after starting `sub` I must hardcode `sleep 0.7` before the first `pub` or the SUB's FIFO is not yet registered and the message is silently fan-out-missed. My fan-out challenge only passed because of that sleep. This is the F5 race the docs admit to, but for an integrator wiring this into a script it is the single biggest ergonomics gap: there is no `--ready` fd, no blocking 'registered' line on sub stdout, nothing to wait on deterministically.
friction_points.workaround: Hardcode a sleep after every sub launch; tune it up under load. Fragile but works.

friction_points.description: Delivery-contract wording vs observed behavior, for a multi-line payload: docs say 'a payload is truncated at its first newline'. When I piped `printf 'line1\nline2\n'` to a single `pub`, the subscriber received BOTH `line1` and `line2` as two separate intact records (not 'line1' with 'line2' dropped). The behavior is arguably better than the doc, but it contradicts the literal 'truncated at first newline' sentence: a multi-line stdin becomes multiple records, it is not truncated. An integrator reading the contract would mis-predict that line2 is lost. Minor honesty/precision gap in the contract wording.
friction_points.workaround: Treat each newline as a record boundary (the actual behavior), not as truncation.

wished_for: A deterministic 'subscriber registered' signal (a line on sub stdout, or a --ready fd) so scripts can wait instead of sleeping.
A clarified one-line delivery-contract sentence: 'each newline in stdin starts a new record' rather than 'truncated at first newline'.
what_worked_well: Core fan-out is exactly right: two weather subs both got 'sunny' intact, traffic sub got nothing, pub rc=0, zero stderr, empty broker.log, no drops.
Topic isolation is filesystem-clean: topics/weather/ and topics/traffic/ are separate dirs; off-topic sub never saw the message.
Glob enumeration (ls topics/weather/sub_*.fifo | wc -l) is stable and scriptable; reflects live count and updates on death (2->1 after killing a sub).
Forget-on-death works: killing a sub pruned its FIFO; surviving sub kept receiving subsequent publishes.

Payload integrity is excellent for an integrator: 4KB and 64KB single-line payloads delivered fully intact (len=4096, len=65536), no torn/concatenated frames; NUL byte stripped cleanly (ab\x00cd -> abcd), spaces/tabs/quotes/$HOME literal preserved.
Publish to a topic with no subscribers is a clean rc=0 no-op, no error spew (matches persona expectation).
comparison_to_expectations: Round-001 was 9/10 for this pair. Round-002 holds at 9/10: every round-001 issue my challenge can reach is confirmed RESOLVED and stayed fixed, and the core fan-out plus payload integrity are clean. Not bumped to 10 because the F5 subscriber-ready race (still punted) is the one thing standing between this and a script I'd trust unattended, and I found a minor contract-wording imprecision (fp-002).
free_text: Black-box re-verification at a82ae2a. All round-001 regressions reachable from a pub/sub-fanout challenge are fixed and clean. F1: bad --at xyz / --delay 1.5 / --delay -3 all return rc=1 with a one-line reason and a completely empty broker.log (no bash errors). F7: --at 99999999999 rejected rc=1, deferred/ stayed empty. H1: pub '../../../tmp/PWNED' rejected rc=1, /tmp/PWNED NOT created, only 'weather' topic dir exists; leading-dot '.hidden' also rejected. F4: sub --help / pub --help print usage and exit 0. H4: ran tests/flood_wedged.sh 3x consecutively, all pass=4 fail=0 in ~4s each, no cold-start hang, max broker-descendants=20 (flat), drops visible (~359-376). F2 doc: 'cat drops_* 2>/dev/null || echo no drops' works on a healthy topic. The one new observation is fp-002 (multi-line stdin yields multiple records, not truncation as the doc literally says). I did not exercise the deadline-scheduler chaos gate (out of scope for this fan-out challenge) so proof_assessment is null.

claim_audit.claim: embedded NUL bytes are stripped, a payload is truncated at its first newline
claim_audit.evidence: NUL stripping confirmed (ab\x00cd delivered as abcd). But a single pub of 'line1\nline2\n' delivered TWO intact records line1 and line2 to the subscriber -- newline acts as a record separator, not a truncation point; line2 was NOT discarded.

claim_audit.claim: Hostile control input is rejected at the boundary ... topic name must be [A-Za-z0-9._-]+ with no leading dot ... returns a nonzero exit with a one-line reason instead of crashing a handler or parking a never-firing record
claim_audit.evidence: ../../../tmp/PWNED rejected rc=1, /tmp/PWNED not created; .hidden rejected rc=1; --at 99999999999 rejected rc=1 with deferred/ empty; bad --at/--delay rc=1 with clean broker.log.

claim_audit.claim: the publisher's process count stays flat (~15)
claim_audit.evidence: tests/flood_wedged.sh reported max broker-descendants=20 across 3 runs (close to documented; flat, not ballooning) while the must-fail control balloons.
